coredata.dc:title: Neutrophil elastase-activated carbon dots for non-invasive early diagnosis of inflammatory bowel disease by near-infrared-II fluorescence imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Deyan
Xiao, Ziwen
Hu, Yaoyu
Wu, Silong
Yi, Zineng
Sheng, Taikui
Zha, Zhengbao
Ma, Yan
Miao, Zhaohua
coredata.dc:description: Most near-infrared-II (NIR-II) fluorescent probes produce an “always-on” output with poor signal specificity for biomarkers. Neutrophil elastase (NE) is a critical factor in chronic inflammation or in the acute response to infection and injury, and NE activatable NIR-II fluorescent probes can aid in the early clinical diagnosis of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). In this paper, we synthesized OPDG-PFA@PEI carbon dots (CDs) through a two-step reaction of solvothermal/surface covalent bond modification, which can “turn on” specific NIR-II fluorescence signals related to the level of NE produced by neutrophils and target sites of inflammation effectively, thus realizing the monitoring of in-situ NE in cells and in vivo. OPDG-PFA@PEI CDs fluorescent nanoprobe show high-performance real-time non-invasive NIR-II fluorescence imaging for early diagnostic evaluation of IBD in mouse models of DSS-induced IBD, providing a promising approach for the development of clinical non-invasive and more convenient early diagnosis of IBD.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Neutrophil elastase
Carbon dots
Non-invasive diagnosis
Near-infrared-II fluorescent nanoprobe
Inflammatory bowel disease

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of OPDG-PFA@PEI by solvothermal reaction and surface covalent bond modification. (a) Schematic synthesis of OPDG-PFA@PEI. (b) TEM image of OPDG. (c) TEM image of OPDG-PFA@PEI (d) XRD pattern of freshly prepared OPDG and OPDG-PFA. (e) Hydrated particle size of OPDG and OPDG-PFA@PEI. (f) ζ potential of OPDG, OPDG-PFA and OPDG-PFA@PEI. (g) Vis-NIR spectra of OPDG and OPDG-PDA@PEI.

Properties of OPDG and OPDG-PFA@PEI. (a) XPS spectra of freshly prepared OPDG-PFA. The core level spectra of (b) C and (c) F elements in OPDG-PFA. (d) Fluorescent spectra of OPDG and OPDG-PFA@PEI excited at 808 nm. (e) Photograph and NIR-II brightness (900 ∼ 1700 nm) of OPDG and OPDG-PFA@PEI, respectively. (f) Laser stability of OPDG and OPDG-PFA@PEI. (g) Schematic of NE-specific recognition and activation of OPDG-PFA@PEI. (h) Selectivity fluorescence intensity of OPDG-PFA@PEI (500 µg/mL) treated with various relevant species (200 μM) and enzymes (750 pg/mL) in PBS buffer (pH 7.4). List of columns: (1) blank; (2) Vitamin C; (3) Cysteine; (4) Glutathione; (5) Glucose; (6) Glycine; (7) Histidine; (8) Leucine; (9) H2S; (10) Fe3+; (11) Cu2+; (12) Mg2+; (13) Zn2+; (14) HClO; (15) H2O2; (16) Bovine albumin; (17) γ-glutamyl transferase; (18) β-Galactosidase; (19) Esterase; (20) NE; (21) artificial gastric fluid; (22) artificial intestinal fluid; (23) pH=1; (24) pH=3; (25) pH=5 (λex = 808 nm, λem = 900–1700 nm).

Biocompatibility of OPDG-PFA@PEI. (a) Fluorescence images of calcein-AM/PI co-stained NCM460 cells after various treatments and (b) Corresponded cell viabilities. (c) Hemolysis test of OPDG-PFA@PEI. (d) Changes in body weight of mice within 14 days after oral administration of OPDG-PFA@PEI. (e) H&E staining of major organs in healthy mice on the 30th day after oral administration of OPDG-PDFA@PEI.

Cellular NE NIR-II fluorescence imaging of Raw 264.7 cells with OPDG-PFA@PEI. (a) Schematic of cell preparation process. (b) Raw 264.7 cells were initially induced with or without LPS for 0, 1, 5, 8 h, followed by incubation with or without sivelestat for 3 h. Subsequently the cells were treated with OPDG-PFA@PEI for 30 min for fluorescence imaging. (c) Quantification of cell fluorescence intensity (fluorescence imaging parameter: λex = 808 nm). (d) NE levels in Raw 264.7 cell cultures detected by ELISA kit. (e ∼ f) Levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and IL-1β in Raw 264.7 cell cultures (*p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, and *** p < 0.001).

Metabolism of OPDG-PFA@PEI. (a) Experimental schedule for assessing acute colitis disease severity by OPDG-PFA@PEI. (b) NIR-II fluorescence imaging in the supine position of mice after OPDG-PFA@PEI oral administration as a function of time (injection dose: 100 mg/kg; imaging conditions: 808 nm laser excitation, 900 nm long-pass filter). (c) NIR-II fluorescence signals in the intestine and liver of OPDG-PFA@PEI during the relevant time period.

NIR-II fluorescence imaging and IBD assessment after OPDG-PFA@PEI oral administration. (a) Body weight changes in each group of mice. (b) DAI scores of each group. (c) NIR-II fluorescence imaging of normal mice, early-stage IBD and advanced IBD after 4 h OPDG-PFA@PEI oral administration. (d) Quantification of fluorescence intensity in the abdomen of mice (statistical ROI area shown in (c)). (e) Colonic length of mice. (f) NIR-II fluorescence imaging of mouse colon in each group. (g) NE levels in the homogenate of mouse colon detected by ELISA kit. (h ∼ j) Levels of TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β in colon homogenates from each group of mice (*p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, and *** p < 0.001). (k) H&E staining of colon tissue from normal mice, early-stage IBD and advanced IBD after 4 h OPDG-PFA@PEI oral administration.
Schematic illustration of the NE-activated carbon dots OPDG-PFA@PEI for non-invasive diagnosis of IBD by NIR-II fluorescence imaging via oral administration.

head.ce:title: Neutrophil elastase-activated carbon dots for non-invasive early diagnosis of inflammatory bowel disease by near-infrared-II fluorescence imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sinopharm Group Guorui Pharmaceutical Co. LTD, Huainan 232001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sinopharm Group Guorui Pharmaceutical Co. LTD

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sinopharm Group Guorui Pharmaceutical Co. LTD, Huainan 232001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering, Anhui Medical University, Hefei 230022, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biomedical Engineering, Anhui Medical University, Hefei 230022, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, PR China (Z. Miao).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: D.G. and Z. X. contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The neutrophil elastase-activated carbon dots OPDG-PFA@PEI exhibit a sensitive NIR-II fluorescence signal enhancement response toward in-situ neutrophil elastase for non-invasive diagnosis of the early-stage inflammatory bowel disease by NIR-II fluorescence imaging via oral administration.

Most near-infrared-II (NIR-II) fluorescent probes produce an “always-on” output with poor signal specificity for biomarkers. Neutrophil elastase (NE) is a critical factor in chronic inflammation or in the acute response to infection and injury, and NE activatable NIR-II fluorescent probes can aid in the early clinical diagnosis of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). In this paper, we synthesized OPDG-PFA@PEI carbon dots (CDs) through a two-step reaction of solvothermal/surface covalent bond modification, which can “turn on” specific NIR-II fluorescence signals related to the level of NE produced by neutrophils and target sites of inflammation effectively, thus realizing the monitoring of in-situ NE in cells and in vivo. OPDG-PFA@PEI CDs fluorescent nanoprobe show high-performance real-time non-invasive NIR-II fluorescence imaging for early diagnostic evaluation of IBD in mouse models of DSS-induced IBD, providing a promising approach for the development of clinical non-invasive and more convenient early diagnosis of IBD.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a complex disease, inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) includes ulcerative colitis and Crohn’s disease, which is distinguished by inflammation and ulceration of the colon mucosal layer and is a high risk for colorectal cancer [1–4]. Early diagnosis and treatment are important for patients with IBD. Research indicates that there is frequently a procrastination in the diagnosis of IBD, and that this delay is associated with detrimental clinical consequences, particularly an increased risk of bowel surgery [5]. Clinical diagnosis of IBD is made by endoscopy [6], radiologic tests such as barium meal X-ray imaging [7], and histologic examination. However, endoscopy is invasive and poses a challenge to patient compliance, whereas radiological imaging carries the risk of exposure to ionizing radiation. Neutrophil elastase (NE) is a serine protease typically expressed in monocytes, T cells, and mast cells [8], which is a critical factor in chronic inflammation and in the acute response to infections and injuries and is a well-known inflammatory sustaining factor and promising prognostic biomarker. Excessive activation and release of NE releases inflammatory factors to promote the development of various inflammation-related diseases. Elastase expression in neutrophils is directly correlated with IBD, and NE is highly expressed at sites of inflammation in patients with IBD [9,10]. Therefore, for accurate clinical diagnosis and drug therapy, rapid and effective methods for detecting IBD-related biomarkers are highly needed for early-stage IBD noninvasive diagnosis.

The challenges of optical imaging for diagnosing IBD are many, such as the autofluorescence of abdominal cavity that can interfere with diagnostic results, and the low depth of imaging penetration in the visible to near-infrared-I (NIR-I, 400–900 nm) fluorescence [11–13]. Carbon dots (CDs), characterized by stable and excellent optical properties, low toxicity, high dispersibility, and low production cost, have received increasing attention in the field of biomaterials [14–16]. The fluorescence emission of most reported fluorescent nanoprobes are limited to the visible to NIR-I region [17–20]. More and more studies have shown that in vivo the near-infrared-II (NIR-II, 1000–3000 nm) fluorescence imaging can significantly enhance the imaging resolution, tissue penetration depth, and high signal-to-noise ratio. This technology can be widely applied in various biomedical fields [21,22]. Consequently, there has been a surge in research interest and application of NIR-II fluorescence imaging [23–27]. The NIR-II fluorescent CDs will potentially raise the profile of NIR-II fluorescence imaging for disease surveillance to new heights.

Several fluorescent sensors with pentafluoropropionic anhydride (PFA) moiety as a leaving group have been explored for the detection of NE, but they do not enable real-time imaging of the NIR-II region and the extent of noninvasive IBD lesions in vivo [9,28]. The NIR-II fluorescence imaging results reported by Shoujun Zhu et al. showed that the NIR-II fluorescent CDs based on DSPE-mPEG2000 encapsulated NIR-II fluorescent CDs, which preferentially accumulate in the inflammatory microenvironment, enabled noninvasive IBD assessment [29]. However, there are relatively few studies on the design of activatable NIR-II fluorescent nanoprobes capable of monitoring NE in vivo. Most NIR-II fluorescent nanoprobes produce an “always-on” output with poor signal specificity for biomarkers. It is crucial to develop activatable probes for monitoring of deep tissue real-time pathological processes at the molecular level [13,20,30–33].

Here, we developed a NE-activated NIR-II fluorescent CDs (OPDG-PFA@PEI) for noninvasive diagnostic evaluation of early-stage IBD by NIR-II fluorescence imaging monitoring of NE activity (Scheme 1 ). OPDG was synthesized by solvothermal method with strong NIR-II fluorescence emission in aqueous solution. OPDG-PFA@PEI is obtained by coupling OPDG fluorophore with PFA moiety as an activatable molecule. OPDG-PFA@PEI has low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, which can be activated and provide specific NIR-II fluorescence enhancement response to NE. OPDG-PFA@PEI is used for the non-invasive diagnostic evaluation of early-stage IBD by NIR-II fluorescence imaging after oral administration. Fluorescence imaging specifically monitored the up-regulated accumulation of NE in the colonic site of IBD mice, providing real-time and dynamic imaging of in-situ NE for the noninvasive diagnosis of IBD.

Pentafluoropropionic anhydride, o-phenylenediamine, glutathione, formamide and dextran sodium sulfate (DSS) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Inc. ELISA kits were purchased from Wuhan Gene Beauty Biotechnology Co., Ltd.. 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. Calcein-AM/PI was purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Company. All reagents were used as received.

OPDG-PFA@PEI was prepared by a two-step solvothermal/surface covalent bond modification reaction. 108 mg of o-phenylenediamine and 15 mg of glutathione were dissolved in 10 mL of formamide, sonicated for 10 min to mix the systems, and then reacted in a hydrothermal reactor at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction was completed, the aqueous OPDG solution was dialyzed using dialysis bags (500 Da) for 24 h. The aqueous solution of OPDG was completely evaporated using a rotary evaporator to obtain carbon dot OPDG.

37.3 mg of OPDG was dissolved in 2 mL DMF, 28 µL of triethylamine was added. Next, 37 μL of pentafluoropropionic anhydride was added slowly in an ice bath. Then the mixture was stirred at 37 °C for 4 h. OPDG-PFA was dried by rotary evaporator. 30 mg of OPDG-PFA was dissolved in 6 mL of dichloromethane, to which 27 mL of an aqueous solution containing 90 mg of PEI was added. The mixture was emulsified by probe sonication for 10 min. Subsequently, the mixture was evaporated using a rotary evaporator to obtain the final product OPDG-PFA@PEI, which was stored away from light.

The morphology of OPDG and OPDG-PFA@PEI was studied by transmission electron microscopy. The elemental valence states of OPDG-PFA were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (ESCALAB250Xi). Near-infrared fluorescence spectra were obtained by Edinburgh FLS980 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (808 nm laser excitation). Fluorescence quantum yields were calculated according to previous literature methods [13]. In order to obtain the fluorescence quantum yields of OPDG and OPDG-PFA@PEI, indocyanine green was used as a standard with a known fluorescence quantum yield value of 13 % in DMSO [34].

NIR-II fluorescence images were acquired by a MonetIGS-1500 Full Spectrum Fluorescence In Vivo Imaging System (School of Biomedical Engineering, Anhui Medical University) (808 nm laser excitation, 900 nm long-pass filter). The fluorescence signal was tracked by a two-dimensional InGaAs camera with 67 mW/cm2 exposure of an 808 nm laser.

NCM460 cells were cultured in modified Roche 1640 culture medium. NCM460 cells were inoculated into 96-well plates for 12 h. Different concentrations of OPDG-PFA@PEI were added. After 24 h incubation, MTT (5 mg/mL) was added for cell viability determination at absorbance of 490 nm.

After NCM460 cells were incubated with OPDG-PFA@PEI containing different concentrations for 24 h, 500 µL of mixed dye (calcein-AM/PI, 15 µL:10 µL) was added and the live/dead staining was observed by fluorescence microscopy.

NE is present in mast cells, T cells and monocytes. Macrophages are derived from monocytes. RAW 264.7 cells (macrophages) were selected as the source of NE. RAW 264.7 cells were divided into 2 groups, the first group was selected to be treated with or without sivelestat (10 µM). The second group was treated with lipopolysaccharide (LPS, 20 μg/mL) for 0 h, 1 h, 5 h, and 8 h respectively, and then treated with sivelestat. After 3 h incubation, the cells were treated with OPDG-PFA@PEI (0.5 mg/mL) for 30 min incubation, and fluorescence imaging (λex = 808 nm, 900 long-pass filter) was obtained by MonetIGS-1500 Full Spectrum Fluorescence In Vivo Imaging System.

6 Weeks old female balb/c mice (16 − 20 g) were purchased from henan sikebas Biotechnology Co., Ltd., and the ambient temperature was controlled between 20 °C and 24 °C. OPDG-PFA@PEI (100 mg/kg) was injected by gavage on day 0, and the body weights of mice were recorded continuously for the next 14 days. Major organs and blood were collected on day 30 for hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining, blood biochemistry analysis, and blood routine analysis.

To establish the IBD mouse model, 35 g of DSS was dissolved in 1 L of deionized water to make a 3.5 % DSS solution. The mice were fed with 3.5 % DSS instead of drinking water in the experimental group for 4 or 7 consecutive days as the early-stage IBD model and the conventional IBD model, respectively, and the body weight changes, fecal bleeding, and visible fecal consistency of the mice were continuously observed and recorded to obtain the early-stage IBD model and the conventional IBD model, respectively. All mice were completely depilated in the abdomen with depilatory cream before the experiment, and the food was replaced with jelly 24 h before the experiment to minimize the interference of mouse chow on the abdominal imaging.

To study the in vivo distribution of OPDG-PFA@PEI, early-stage IBD mice were randomly divided into 6 groups (n = 3) and orally administered a suspension of OPDG-PFA@PEI (100 mg/kg). Supine position of mice NIR-II fluorescence images were acquired at predetermined time intervals (0 h, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 12 h, 24 h, 48 h) using the MonetIGS-1500 full-spectrum fluorescence in vivo imaging system (under 808 nm excitation using a 900 long-pass filter). Early-stage IBD model and conventional IBD model were established with 3.5 % DSS, and healthy mice were used as control group, which were orally administered OPDG-PFA@PEI (100 mg/kg) suspension, respectively. Values are mean ± SD (n = 3). After imaging, the colon was dissected and collected for pathological analysis. For assessment of inflammation levels, TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, myeloperoxidase (MPO) and NE of colon homogenates were quantified by commercial ELISA kits.

A two-step method including a solvothermal reaction and a surface covalent bond modification reaction was used for the synthesis of OPDG-PFA@PEI. In the first step, OPDG was synthesized by solvothermal method using inexpensive and readily available raw materials. OPDG synthesis was optimized by varying the ratio of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to GSH dosage. The synthesis of OPDG was optimized by varying the OPD to GSH dosing ratio. Fig. S1 showed that OPDG had the maximum fluorescence intensity at 800 nm (λex = 725 nm) with a molar ratio of OPD/GSH=1:0.05, which was selected as the optimal synthesis scheme. In the second step, OPDG-PFA was synthesized through a surface covalent bond modification reaction between OPDG and PFA groups, and then encapsulated with polyethylenimine (PEI) to obtain OPDG-PFA@PEI in order to improve its aqueous dispersibility (Fig. 1 a). According to the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism, PFA quench the fluorescence of fluorophore (OPDG), OPDG-PFA display weak fluorescence, after the amide bond cleaved by NE, strong fluorescence of OPDG would produce [35,36]. The combination of PFA and OPDG through amide bonding can quench or attenuate the CDs fluorescence intensity.

The morphology of OPDG, OPDG-PFA@PEI was analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), in which OPDG and OPDG-PFA@PEI showed a particle shape with a diameter of ∼ 5 nm and ∼ 150 nm, respectively (Fig. 1b, c). The larger size of OPDG-PFA@PEI suggests the aggregates formation of some OPDG-PFA encapsulated within PEI. The broad peak at 2θ = 22° in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern revealed the graphitic structure in OPDG and OPDG-PFA (Fig. 1d). The hydrated particle size of OPDG measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) was about 8 nm, and the hydrated particle size of OPDG-PFA@PEI modified by PFA and PEI was about 160 nm (Fig. 1e). The zeta potentials of OPDG and OPDG-PFA were −8.06 ± 0.85 mV and −10.21 ± 0.89 mV, respectively, and that of OPDG-PFA@PEI had a zeta potential of 40.2 ± 1.18 mV (Fig. 1f), indicating that the positively charged PEI was successfully modified on the surface of OPDG-PFA. As shown in Fig. 1g, OPDG has significant absorption range from 500-1100 nm, which was much larger than that of OPDG-PFA@PEI at the same concentration.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra showed five peaks (Fig. 2 a) attributed to S2p (0.92 at.%), C1s (54.06 at.%), N1s (21.23 at.%), O1s (18.74 at.%) and F1s (5.05 at.%). High-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 2b, S2) showed the presence of four types of carbon in OPDG-PFA, including C=C, C-C and C-O, and C-N. High-resolution F1s spectra (Fig. 2c) showed the presence of C-F, which is derived from PFA, in OPDG-PFA. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) showed the presence of C-F, which is derived from PFA, in OPDG-PFA, as compared to OPDG, characteristic peak of C-F at 1212 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S3). All of the above data indicated that PFA was successfully modified onto OPDG. As shown in Fig. 2d, NIR-II fluorescence spectra of OPDG and OPDG-PFA@PEI (808 nm excitation) showed that OPDG had a strong emission peak at 1091 nm, and it is noteworthy that the OPDG fluorescence signal was significantly higher than that of OPDG-PFA@PEI. Similarly, OPDG strong NIR-II fluorescence signal was observed in the full-spectrum fluorescence in vivo imaging system, and the NIR-II fluorescence signal of OPDG-PFA@PEI was weak (Fig. 2e). This implies that OPDG-PFA@PEI has the potential to be applied to NE-activated NIR-II fluorescent probes. The fluorescence quantum yields of OPDG and OPDG-PFA@PEI in aqueous solution were determined to be 1.2 % and 0.3 %, respectively, according to previous literature methods [13], and the quantum yield of OPDG was higher than the reported carbon nanotubes (0.4 %) [37]. Photostability is an important property of fluorescent probes for NIR-II fluorescence imaging, which is directly related to the efficiency and accuracy of imaging. We used 808 excitation light to continuously excite OPDG and OPDG-PFA@PEI for 10 min. The results showed that there was no obvious attenuation of OPDG and OPDG-PFA@PEI NIR-II fluorescence intensity, which had good laser stability (Fig. 2f).

Previous studies have shown that PFA is an NE-specific recognition motif [9,28], and we speculate that NE can recognize and decompose OPDG-PFA@PEI, which dislodges its surface-modifying PFA and activate its intense fluorescence (Fig. 2g). To explore whether OPDG-PFA@PEI function as a specific NIR-II fluorescence response to NE, various bioactive analytes were selected to test the selective recognition properties of OPDG-PFA@PEI. As shown in Fig. 2h, as expected, OPDG-PFA@PEI could be specifically activated by NE, releasing strong NIR-II fluorescence; and OPDG-PFA@PEI showed little fluorescence intensity response to other interfering analytes.

Despite the favorable biocompatibility of CDs, we are interested in evaluating the biotoxicity of OPDG-PFA@PEI before using it for in vivo applications. OPDG-PFA@PEI cytotoxicity assay was performed on NCM460 cells by dead-viable staining assay and MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 3 a, b, negligible dead cells were found in either OPDG or OPDG-PFA@PEI treated groups. The cell viability of NCM 460 was still above 80 % with 100 µg/mL OPDG-PFA@PEI treated, indicating low cytotoxicity. The hemolysis assay showed that when the concentration of OPDG-PFA@PEI reached 200 µg/mL, the hemolysis rate was still below 5 % (Fig. 3c), indicating that OPDG-PFA@PEI has good biocompatibility.

In addition, we performed an in vivo biosafety assessment by administering OPDG and OPDG-PFA@PEI (injection dose: 100 mg/kg) orally to mice, observing and recording the body weights of the mice for 14 consecutive days (Fig. 3d), and using PBS-treated mice as controls. We found no significant difference in body weights of the orally administered OPDG and OPDG-PFA@PEI mice compared with the control group. In addition, we collected organs and blood from mice after 30 days, respectively. Pathological analysis of the organs from normal mice and OPDG-PFA@PEI treated mice also confirmed that OPDG-PFA@PEI have no apparent tissue toxicity (Fig. 3e). Fig. S4a, b demonstrated that there were no significant differences in routine blood indices, such as hemoglobin (HGB), red blood cells (RBCs), mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC), and white blood cells (WBCs), between the control and OPDG-PFA@PEI groups. In addition, typical liver and kidney function indicators, including aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), urea, and total protein (TP) (Fig. S4c-f), were also not significantly different between the different groups, suggesting that there was no significant hepatorenal toxicity of OPDG-PFA@PEI. In conclusion, OPDG-PFA@PEI did not cause significant toxicity in vitro and in vivo, which lays the foundation for the fluorescence imaging in vivo.

Raw 264.7 cells were selected for cellular NE imaging. RAW 264.7 cells were induced with LPS (20 μg/mL) to produce an inflammatory response and incubated for different times. Meanwhile, The OPDG-PFA@PEI (500 μg/mL) were added into cells with LPS-induced/no LPS-induced for 30 min incubation, followed by NIR-II fluorescence imaging (Fig. 4 a). The results showed that the NIR-II fluorescence imaging intensity of inflammatory cells was significantly increased after cells were preincubated with OPDG-PFA@PEI and enhanced with the increase of LPS induction time, and the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum at 5 h. In addition, it was found that the fluorescence signals of the cells preincubated without sivelestat were significantly increased (Fig. 4b). Analyzing of cellular fluorescence intensity with ImageJ showed that the NIR-II fluorescence imaging signal intensity values of cells induced by LPS for 5 h were approximately 3-fold higher than those of the control, and cellular NE secretion was maximal at 5 h of LPS induction. The fluorescence of sivelestat cells group was significantly lower than that of cells not preincubated with sivelestat (Fig. 4c), indicating that NE, whose activity was inhibited was unable to activate the NIR-II fluorescence of OPDG-PFA@PEI. This result suggests that OPDG-PFA@PEI can be applied as cellular NE-specific NIR-II fluorescent probe. Using a commercialized ELISA kit, we further confirmed the expression of NE in RAW 264.7 cells after LPS treatment. The results showed that NE levels were significantly elevated at 1 h of LPS induction compared to the blank control group, and reached a higher level at 5 h of induction time (Fig. 4d). This suggests that NE can be overexpressed in RAW 264.7 cells stimulated by LPS. We further evaluated the level of LPS-induced cellular inflammatory response and found that the IL-6, IL-1β and TNF-α levels were significantly enhanced in the LPS-treated RAW 264.7 cell culture medium (Fig. 4e-f, S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NIR-II fluorescence imaging for diagnosis of IBD
ce:section.ce:para: The IBD model was established by oral administration of DSS. Early-stage IBD was induced by treating mice drinking water containing 3.5 % DSS for 4 consecutive days, and advanced IBD was induced for 7 consecutive days. NIR-II fluorescence imaging experiments were performed by MonetIGS-1500 Full Spectrum Fluorescence In Vivo Imaging System for the early-stage IBD and advanced IBD, respectively (Fig. 5 a). Oral administration is considered more appropriate for clinical practice in the treatment of gastrointestinal diseases compared to other administration. Oral administration was chosen to evaluate the metabolism of OPDG-PFA@PEI. Early-stage IBD model mice were subjected to NIR-II fluorescence imaging at different time points to observe their metabolic behaviors after oral administration of OPDG-PFA@PEI (dosage: 100 mg/kg) (Fig. 5b). OPDG-PFA@PEI were activated by NE in the gastrointestinal tract of early-stage IBD model mice, and OPDG-PFA@PEI metabolites inhibit a significant NIR-II fluorescence signal enhancement. In-situ NE could be stable and noninvasively monitored via NIR-II fluorescence imaging. Mouse intestinal and liver fluorescence intensity values were plotted relative to the time points after oral administration (Fig. 5c), and it was found that the fluorescence signal at the liver site peaked at the time points of 4 and 12 h, respectively, indicating a classic enterohepatic cycling process. OPDG-PFA@PEI metabolites are partially metabolized and excreted into the bile during their first passage through the liver, and subsequently enter the intestine, where they are again absorbed and returned to the liver, resulting in a second peak. OPDG-PFA@PEI showed a hepatobiliary-fecal clearance pathway. In addition, we observed that the fluorescence intensity in the intestine of mice reached a maximum value at 4 h. Therefore, 4 h was determined to be the most appropriate time point for assessing the extent of early-stage IBD disease, and OPDG-PFA@PEI has the potential to enable non-invasive early diagnosis of IBD by NIR-II fluorescence imaging via oral administration. Moreover, the time-dependent fluorescence signal in normal mice after OPDG-PFA@PEI administration were monitored. As shown in Fig. S6, OPDG-PFA@PEI were slightly activated by low-level NE in the gastrointestinal tract of normal mice on the 4 h time point, and the fluorescence intensity in the liver of mice reached peak value after 12 h metabolism.

We further systematically validated the non-invasive diagnosis of the early-stage IBD functional properties of OPDG-PFA@PEI. We chose to perform NIR-II fluorescence imaging experiments on normal mice, early-stage IBD mice, and late-stage IBD mice. Prior to imaging, we observed and recorded the body weights of the mice in each group. Compared with the control mice, the mice that treated with the 3.5 % DSS aqueous solution for four days showed only a weak tendency to decrease in body weight, whereas the mice that treated with the 3.5 % DSS aqueous solution for seven days were observed to have a drastic weight loss (Fig. 6 a). The IBD model was additionally evaluated through the classical disease activity index (DAI), which includes weight loss, fecal consistency and fecal bleeding. DAI values of 3.5 % DSS treated mice were significantly elevated compared to the control group, suggesting that the model was successfully constructed in all groups of mice (Fig. 6b).

The mice in each group were orally administered OPDG-PFA@PEI (injection dose: 100 mg/kg), and the imaging results were obtained by NIR-II fluorescence imaging after 4 h. We found that bright NIR-II fluorescence signals were detected in the intestines of both early-stage IBD mice and advanced IBD mice orally administered with OPDG-PFA@PEI, while the fluorescence signals in the intestines of normal mice were weak (Fig. 6c). The NIR-II fluorescence signal (>900 nm) intensity of mouse abdomen was determined by the region of interest (ROI) analyzing using ImageJ, and the results displayed that the intensity of NIR-II fluorescent signal in the intestines of early-stage IBD mice and advanced IBD mice were about 6 times higher than that of the control group (Fig. 6d). To compare the disease screening ability of OPDG-PFA@PEI with that of clinical/preclinical methods, we further dissected mice for colon length, histologic examination, and cytokine detection. Fig. 6e showed that colon length in mice with intestinal lesions showed a significant shortening trend with increasing days of DSS administration. The dissected mouse colon was also subjected to NIR-II fluorescence imaging, and the colon fluorescence intensity was analyzed with ImageJ. Consistent with the expected results, the colon fluorescence intensity was significantly higher in early-stage IBD mice and advanced IBD mice than in the control group (Fig. 6f, S7), which further confirms that OPDG-PFA@PEI can be specifically recognized by the up-regulated NE at the colonic site of IBD mice and activated, releasing strong fluorescence. The MPO and pro-inflammatory cytokines levels of the DSS-treated mice colon appeared to be significantly elevated after 4 days and reached even higher levels on day 7 (Fig. 6g-j, S8), which verified the intensified inflammatory response of IBD in mice with the increase in the number of days of DSS administration. Histological analysis showed colonic epithelial damage, submucosal edema, and inflammatory cell infiltration in the 7-day DSS-treated group of mice (Fig. 6k), whereas no significant lesions were observed in the control and 4-day DSS-treated groups, suggesting that histological examination could not achieve the screening of the early-stage IBD. These data highlight the fact that OPDG-PFA@PEI can achieve the non-invasive diagnosis of the early-stage IBD and that OPDG-PFA@PEI NIR-II fluorescence imaging of early-stage IBD is more accurate and convenient for noninvasive diagnosis than colonoscopy and intestinal histologic analysis.

To address many of the challenges of diagnosing IBD by optical imaging techniques, we designed and synthesized OPDG-PFA@PEI fluorescent nanoprobe by a two-step solvothermal/surface covalent bond modification reaction, which can be applied for NIR-II fluorescence imaging. The emission centers of water-soluble CDs are formed by covalently conjugated carbon core. OPDG-PFA@PEI is prepared by a simple synthesis procedure, low cost of reaction precursors and good photostability. OPDG-PFA@PEI exhibit a sensitive NIR-II fluorescence signal enhancement response at 1091 nm after being activated by NE. Impressively, after oral administration, OPDG-PFA@PEI is specifically activated by highly expressed NE at the site of inflammation. NIR-II fluorescence imaging of in-situ NE activity enables screening the early-stage IBD with negligible side effects in mouse models of DSS-induced IBD, which is superior to histologic examination and other typical clinical tests. Further studies of activatable NIR-II fluorescent CDs will help to improve clinical optical non-invasive diagnostic techniques.

Deyan Gong: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ziwen Xiao: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yaoyu Hu: Visualization, Validation, Methodology. Silong Wu: Visualization, Methodology. Zineng Yi: Methodology, Investigation. Taikui Sheng: Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Zhengbao Zha: Validation, Supervision, Resources. Yan Ma: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhaohua Miao: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has received approval for research ethics from Hefei University of Technology and a proof/certificate of approval is available upon request (HFUT20240108003). Research was afforded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82202221, 82370562, 82170572), the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (2208085QB39), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JZ2024HGTG0320).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
